author: Player3
content:
feat(cert:[516]): Witt Tower discrimination ladder AR(1)-baseline audit
Companion audit of certs [490]-[495] (rivers/precip/temperature/SST/EEG/FX
discrimination ladder), triggered by noticing the memory-index summary of
this chain listed the ratios in an order that wasn't actually descending
(precip 3.05 placed ahead of temp 3.40) -- a transcription typo, fixed in
memory, but it prompted checking the underlying claim itself rather than
just the ordering.

The ladder ranks domains by raw n_signal_ratio (observed / expected-under-
independence). That conflates two different things: how strongly a domain
is ALREADY autocorrelated at lag 1 (a pre-existing, textbook fact for each
domain -- ocean thermal inertia, synoptic blocking, cited in the certs'
own primary sources) vs. how much the QA rank-bin operator reveals BEYOND
that known autocorrelation.

Built a plain AR(1)/correlated-Gaussian Monte Carlo simulator, ran it at
each domain's own reported lag-1 autocorrelation (pooled from each cert's
certified fallback/artifact data; live-fetched from USGS NWIS for rivers,
whose fallback record doesn't store an autocorr field), through the
IDENTICAL rank-bin operator the real certs use. Result: SST (rho=0.942)
and temperature (rho=0.728) -- presented as "STRONGEST PERSISTENCE" -- are
essentially fully explained by plain correlation at their own rho
(excess_ratio 1.05 and 1.01: a bare AR(1) process already predicts 4.23x
and 3.36x, nearly matching the observed 4.432x and 3.400x). EEG interictal
(1.64), precipitation (1.60), rivers (1.43), and FX (1.42) -- ranked lower
or "null" in the original framing -- show substantial genuine excess
beyond what plain correlation predicts. Reranking by excess_ratio instead
of raw ratio nearly inverts the published ladder.

This does not retract [490]-[495]'s underlying data or n_signal_ratio
computations, independently re-verified here as correct -- it corrects
the interpretive framing that implicitly equated "highest raw ratio" with
"most novel finding," when the two only coincide for a domain with
near-zero baseline autocorrelation.

Scaffolded as a full cert family: 9 fast, deterministic gating checks
(lookups against the recorded empirical values, not a Monte Carlo re-run
on every self-test), 9 pass + 2 fail fixtures, mapping_protocol_ref.json,
docs/families entry, qa_meta_validator.py registration, and a standalone
reproduce_ar1_baseline.py (pure Python, no dependencies; verified to
reproduce the cited numbers exactly, including a --fetch-rivers flag to
re-derive the live USGS autocorrelation).

Refreshed qa_kg_determinism_cert_v1/expected_hash.json (documented
remedy) since the new FAMILY_SWEEPS entry legitimately changed the
QA-KG graph hash -- same gotcha as the [496] and [515] commits.

Full qa_meta_validator.py suite: 0 FAIL.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
feat(cert:[515]): QA orbit-derived NTRU keys weak when modulus divisible by 3
New cert family, not an extension of existing work. Audited informal
chat-derived "QARSDC"/"QAFST" cryptography material that claimed QA
"harmonic class filtering" mitigates lattice attacks -- found no actual
lattice basis was ever specified (literally "L=span{...}", the ellipsis
never filled in) and one entry explicitly reported fpylll as unavailable.
No real lattice attack had ever been run against these constructions.

Built a real NTRU-lattice implementation (ring Z[x]/(x^N-1), standard
2N-dim attack lattice), verified it algebraically correct, installed real
fpylll, and ran actual LLL/BKZ attacks comparing random ternary keys
against QA-orbit-derived ternary keys (coefficient = (b mod 3) - 1).

Result: QA-orbit keys are catastrophically weaker than random at
parameter sizes where random resists (N=83: random 0/10 broken by LLL,
QA m=9 6/10 broken with ratios as low as 0.01-0.24) -- but only when the
QA modulus is divisible by 3. Proved the exact mechanism: whenever 3|m,
(x mod m) === x (mod 3), so the mod-3 residue of the orbit evolves
exactly as the direct mod-3 recursion, which has intrinsic period at
most 8 regardless of m's own period -- collapsing any polynomial of
degree N>8 into a catastrophically periodic sequence.

Caught and corrected a confounded intermediate hypothesis during
derivation: "short period alone causes it" appeared confirmed by one
comparison (m=9 weak vs m=50 strong) but a proper 2x2 design (period x
gcd(m,3)) falsified it -- both "long period" cases split identically by
gcd(m,3) instead. Confirmed the fix (gcd(m,3)=1) restores full security
under both LLL and BKZ, consistent in both directions (both resist LLL;
both break equally under BKZ at this toy N).

Scaffolded as a full cert family per project convention: validator with
stdlib-only deterministic checks (the mod-3 collapse identity is a
proof, verified exactly for m in {9,15,21,30,300}, not re-run via fpylll
on every self-test), 7 pass + 2 fail fixtures, mapping_protocol_ref.json,
docs/families entry, qa_meta_validator.py registration, and a standalone
reproduce_fpylll_experiment.py (verified to reproduce the cited numbers)
since fpylll is a heavy non-stdlib dependency not assumed to be installed
everywhere this validator runs.

Refreshed qa_kg_determinism_cert_v1/expected_hash.json (documented
remedy) since the new FAMILY_SWEEPS entry legitimately changed the
QA-KG graph hash -- same gotcha as the [496] downgrade commit.

Full qa_meta_validator.py suite: 0 FAIL.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
chore(cert:[228]): refresh determinism fixture after [496] registry edit
The [496] downgrade commit (88062a00) changed qa_meta_validator.py's
FAMILY_SWEEPS claim text for cert 496, which feeds directly into the
QA-KG canonical graph as node content (tools/qa_kg/extractors/certs.py).
That legitimately changed the graph_hash, which showed up as a D2
"in-process rebuild idempotent" drift in cert [228]'s determinism check
when running the full qa_meta_validator.py suite as a final sanity pass.

This is exactly the documented gotcha (CLAUDE.md: "Refresh
qa_kg_determinism_cert_v1/expected_hash.json on any FAMILY_SWEEPS edit")
and the fix is the file's own documented remedy: delete expected_hash.json
and rerun qa_meta_validator.py, which bootstrap-writes the correct
per-platform hash. Also picks up the routine _meta_ledger.json git_head/
timestamp refresh from that run.

Full qa_meta_validator.py suite: 0 FAIL (was 1 FAIL before this refresh,
unrelated to any other cert's correctness).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
fix(cert:[496]): downgrade E8 Satellite Chamber theorem, retract 3 checks
Independently re-verified cert [496]'s claims rather than trusting the
original 2026-06-21 derivation. The (6,3) anchor-selection criterion
held up (it is genuinely the unique Satellite-orbit member whose reduced
triple is the fundamental (3,4,5) -- three other members also satisfy
C<F alone, so that part of the original justification was incomplete
but the conclusion was right).

The branch/Grant-LRT/elementary-uniqueness claims did not hold up.
branch_and_distances() returns an index into the *filtered list of
simple roots* -- order depends on which of the 240 globally-enumerated
E8 roots pass a positivity filter and where they land in that fixed
enumeration -- not a Satellite-axis index. Verified directly: for h=G,
the actual branch (degree-3) root is (0,0,2,0,-2,0,0,0), touching axes
2 and 4 (SAT[2]=(9,3), SAT[4]=(3,6)/Grant LRT) -- not axis 0=(6,3) as
claimed. Not fixable by correcting the index: 7 of the 8 simple roots
in this chamber are Type-2 (nonzero at all 8 coordinates at once), so
there is no well-defined sense in which one axis "is" the branch node
or sits "at distance k" from it.

Retracted ESC_BRANCH, ESC_GRANT, ESC_ELEM_UNIQUE (computed for audit,
no longer gate PASS/FAIL). The remaining 8 checks (ESC_PYTH, ESC_PARITY,
ESC_ROOTS, ESC_GRAM, ESC_WALL_LOWER/UPPER, ESC_ISO_INTERVAL,
ESC_G2_EXITS) do not depend on this axis-to-branch mapping -- they
compare root-projection signs or full simple-root sets directly -- and
remain valid, independently re-verified. Also verified the 4
wall_constraint fixtures (pass_01/02, fail_01/02) happen to be correct
despite using the same branch==0 proxy, by comparing against a proper
same-simple-root-set check directly.

Updated: cert validator (docstring + check functions + self_test +
--self-test JSON output), mapping_protocol_ref.json scope_note, family
doc, and qa_meta_validator.py registry entries, all consistently.

Verified: cert self-test and fixture validation both pass (8/8 gating
checks, 3 retracted checks reported for audit only); qa_mapping_protocol_ref
validator passes; axiom linter clean (0 errors).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
experiments(geometry): fix derive_from_f_qa_mpqs missing Fermat fast-path
Root-caused the pre-existing --stress mismatches (semiprime_10007_10009,
semiprime_1000003_1000033): derive_from_f_qa_mpqs was the only sibling of
derive_from_f_qa_qs and derive_from_f_qa_mpqs_auto without a Fermat
fast-path pre-check. For a balanced semiprime (10007*10009, an easy
Fermat case — d=10008 solves it in 0 steps), the standalone qa-mpqs
method instead relied entirely on its own sieve/dependency-extraction
pipeline finding a *nontrivial* congruence by chance, which it isn't
guaranteed to do (not every null-space dependency yields a nontrivial
gcd, and the sieve's polynomial charts don't reliably hit a multiple of
the true factors just because the factorization happens to be easy).
Confirmed via an in-memory patch test before editing the real file: 32
dependencies were checked for F=100160063 and none exposed the factor,
while qa_fermat_factor solves it in a single check.

Added the same Fermat fast-path used by the sibling functions (matching
this function's own qa_engine schema for consistency), using
factor_large_component() rather than prime_factors_trial() for the
discovered-factor cofactor split, to avoid the unbounded-trial-division
hang risk fixed elsewhere in this file.

Verified: plain --stress now passes 6/6 (previously 4/6); --stress
--stress-auto and --self-test still pass with no regressions; direct CLI
check on F=100160063 confirms both expected candidates now returned.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
experiments(geometry): switch qa_fermat_factor to mod-8-only pruning (1.4-2.5x faster)
Investigated whether extending the residue-pruning modulus beyond the
current mod-24 (=8*3) would speed up qa_fermat_factor's scan, since a
larger modulus prunes more candidates on average. The naive answer
(bigger modulus = free speedup) turned out to be wrong: it assumed
pruning strength alone determines cost, but profiling showed
is_square() only costs ~3x a single residue-filter check — so extending
the modulus (mod 72/120/360/5040/30240, tested via CRT-verified
per-prime-power component filters) makes the *filter itself* more
expensive per candidate by more than it saves in skipped is_square calls.
Confirmed empirically: every tested combination (mod 144, 720, 5040,
30240, 55440) was slower than the current mod-24, not faster.

The actual finding runs the other way: the current mod-24 filter is
itself over-invested. Dropping the mod-3 component and using mod-8 alone
(weaker pruning, 0.375 vs 0.25 avg valid fraction, but half the checks
per candidate) is consistently faster — 1.4-2.5x across 8 independently
constructed hard (imbalanced-factor) test cases, verified on the actual
production qa_fermat_factor, not just a benchmark harness.

Added _VALID_D_RESIDUES_8 (mod-8 table) and switched qa_fermat_factor to
it. Left qa_pollard_factor's seed selection on the existing mod-24 table
(_VALID_D_RESIDUES) unchanged — that's a different use (heuristic seed
diversity, not scan pruning) that would need its own dedicated
verification before changing.

Verified: self-test, --stress --stress-auto still pass; the F%4==2
fast-reject still resolves instantly (the impossibility is a mod-4 fact,
fully captured by mod-8 alone, no mod-3 component needed there either).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
experiments(geometry): add ECM stage-2 continuation, re-tune defaults
Stage 1 alone only catches curve orders that are fully B1-smooth. Added
the standard ECM continuation (_ecm_stage2): after stage 1 produces a
point Q, extend it by one further prime q in (B1, B2] using incremental
additions of precomputed small-gap multiples of Q (scalar multiplication
is a group homomorphism, so q_i*Q = gap*Q + q_(i-1)*Q — no fresh O(log q)
scalar multiply per candidate prime, just ~1 cheap point-add each, reusing
_ec_add's existing gcd-failure factor discovery).

Re-benchmarked with stage 2 available: b1=20000/b2=2,000,000/curves=30
hits 100%/100% success on the same 13/15-digit test batteries that the
old no-stage-2 defaults (b1=20000/curves=50) only got 92%/80% on — fewer
curves needed because each one now covers more ground, so it's both
stronger and cheaper on average (worst-case cost also dropped, ~14s vs
~19-23s, since fewer curves run in the no-solution case). Adopted as the
new default for the qa-mpqs-auto bridge stage and standalone qa-ecm
method. Added --ecm-b2 CLI flag; self_test's lightweight override now
also pins b2==b1 to fully disable stage 2 for its correctness-only check.

Verified: self-test (0.38s) and --stress --stress-auto still pass; CLI
smoke test confirms --ecm-b2 threads through end-to-end.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
experiments(geometry): fix qa_fermat_factor infinite loop, add difference-of-squares fast reject
F = d*d - e*e has an integer solution iff F is odd or F % 4 == 0 (s=d-e,
t=d+e share parity since s+t=2d is even). F % 4 == 2 is therefore provably
unsolvable — and _VALID_D_RESIDUES already encoded this: every F%24
residue class with f % 4 == 2 comes back as an empty frozenset.

qa_fermat_factor's scan loop only incremented its `checked` counter inside
the `if d % 24 in valid` branch. When valid is empty (exactly the F % 4 ==
2 case), that branch never runs, checked never advances, and d climbs
forever — a genuine infinite loop, confirmed via faulthandler.dump_traceback_later
hanging on F=6, F=10, F=1000000000006, etc.

Fix: added difference_of_squares_possible(F) and an explicit `if not
valid: return set()` guard in qa_fermat_factor once the residue class is
known empty. Also short-circuits derive_from_f_qa_mpqs_auto at the top so
provably-impossible F returns instantly instead of also burning Pollard's
rho / ECM / MPQS profile budgets on a search space that's certified
empty. Added a self-test regression guard (F=6,10,14,22,10^12+6, must
resolve in <1s) so this can't silently regress.

Verified: self-test and --stress --stress-auto still pass; the five
impossible-F test cases now resolve in microseconds instead of hanging.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
experiments(geometry): tune ECM defaults for better auto-pipeline success odds
Empirically benchmarked qa_ecm_factor against random 13/15-digit target
primes (the exact ">10^13" gap this bridge targets) across several (b1,
max_curves) settings. The original defaults (b1=2000, curves=25) only
found the factor 25% of the time at 13 digits. b1=20000/curves=50 gets
92%/80% at 13/15 digits, with bounded worst-case cost (~6-19s) before
falling through to MPQS — adopted as the new default for both the
qa-mpqs-auto bridge stage and the standalone qa-ecm method.

Threaded ecm_b1/ecm_max_curves through derive_from_f_qa_mpqs_auto,
derive_from_f, and the CLI (--ecm-b1/--ecm-max-curves) so callers can
override. self_test() now passes explicit lightweight values (b1=200,
curves=5) instead of inheriting the production defaults — it only needs
correctness (ECM finding nothing on tiny primes still falls through to
guaranteed MPQS), not success-rate strength, so it stays fast (0.38s)
rather than paying the tuned defaults' cost on every run.

Verified: self-test and --stress --stress-auto still pass at prior speed;
CLI flags confirmed end-to-end on F=1001.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
experiments(geometry): add Lenstra ECM bridge to the Pythagorean auto-factoring pipeline
Closes the gap noted in the 2026-05-30 stress run: Pollard's rho tops out
around 10^9-10^10, MPQS handles everything but is slow to reach for, and
factors in between (e.g. >10^13, B1-smooth) had no fast path. Adds
qa_ecm_factor (Lenstra ECM, stage 1) with affine EC point arithmetic
(_ec_add/_ec_multiply/_mod_inverse), wired into derive_from_f_qa_mpqs_auto
between Pollard and the MPQS profile loop, plus a standalone --method
qa-ecm / derive_from_f_qa_ecm for direct use with stronger parameters.

Also fixes a real hang this exposed: handing a large ECM/Pollard-discovered
cofactor to prime_factors_trial() does unbounded trial division up to its
square root, which never finishes for a 20+ digit prime cofactor. Added
_is_probable_prime (Miller-Rabin) and factor_large_component() (bounded
trial division + primality gate + one extra rho/ECM round) and swapped it
in wherever the auto pipeline hands off a discovered factor.

Verified: EC arithmetic checked against a brute-force GF(97) reference;
self-test (12 methods x 4 F) and --stress --stress-auto (6/6) still pass
at prior speeds; a constructed 36-digit unbalanced semiprime (15-digit x
22-digit primes) now factors correctly via qa-ecm in ~28s instead of
hanging.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
feat(qa_ml): NT-compliant zero-float BitNet training matches/beats float baseline
Investigates whether QA's discreteness axioms (S2 permits Fraction/int
state, Theorem NT forbids float-causal QA state) offer a legitimate
alternative to BitNet b1.58's ternary-weight training, or whether the
resemblance is surface-level.

Chain of 6 experiments (73-78): a naive integer-vote strawman fails
(10.3%, chance) but this says nothing about discreteness -- rebuilding
with real backprop in exact fixed-point arithmetic (zero floats anywhere
in the causal path) reaches 89.7%. qa_step is bit-identical to plain
subtraction when it doesn't wrap, but actively harmful when it does
(collapses to chance vs. clip's 91%). Integer-analog Adam (exact isqrt
via Newton's method) reaches 91.1% after fixing three low-precision Adam
bugs. Real softmax cross-entropy (integer exp via range-reduced Taylor
series) initially hits a diagnosed expressivity ceiling; adding BitNet's
gamma rescale fixes it but introduces an unbounded positive-feedback
instability caught by a generalization check on MNIST -- traced to a
missing AdamW default (weight_decay=0.01), not the gamma-gradient path
first hypothesized (tested and ruled out directly).

Final, cross-validated result: fixed-point (zero-float) training beats
float BitNet-style STE+AdamW on digits (96.4% vs 94.3%, 300ep/5 seeds)
and matches it on MNIST-6k (92.6% vs 92.8%, 45ep/3 seeds).

Full narrative: docs/specs/QA_ML_BITNET_NT_COMPLIANT_TRAINING_FINDINGS.md

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
feat(igp24): batches 30-36, cyclotomic strategy pivot (700 polys)
Key findings from batch30 analysis:
- polsubcyclo(m,8)×S3 → baseline T-numbers (24T27/30/32) → mostly 0 score
- cyclotomic seed × shift → non-baseline (24T14744/14745/10258) → 1.0 per pair
- Cyclotomic strategy 44× more efficient at discovering new (T,r) pairs

Strategy from batch36: exclusively cyclotomic seeds, covering all underserved
r-buckets: r=4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20,24 (skip r=22, fully saturated).

PARI fix: all if() statements on single lines (multi-line stdin breaks PARI parser).
polsubcyclo() without 'x variable arg (avoids tick-quote/newline parser bug).

7 submissions today, 700 polys total.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Player3
content:
feat(igp24): batch28 — r=0 polys from complex degree-8/12 seeds, more cyclotomic
New T-numbers and r-values:
- A: polsubcyclo(15,8) × S3 → (C4×C2)×S3, r=0 (9 polys, new T+r)
- B: polsubcyclo(24,8) × S3 → (E8)×S3, r=0 (9 polys, new T+r)
- C: D4-quartic-0real × S3 compositum with x²-d → (S3×D4)×C2 at r=0 (12 polys)
- D: S3×V4 f12 compositum with x²-d → (S3×V4)×C2 at r=24 (4 polys, new T)
- E: cyclotomic m=780,840,910,924,...,2520 shift trick (65 polys, 11 new T-numbers)

Submission: sub_1d547238627b427cbdd177dc52401b5e
30/100 polys at r=0 (rare) — new strategy covering complex-signature seeds

author: Player3
content:
feat(igp24): batch26/27 generators — fixed PARI VEC handling, new T-numbers
Key fixes:
- Replace PARI for-loops with apply() in get_totally_real_subcyclo():
  for(i=1,#v,...) fails via subprocess; apply(p->..., v) works correctly
- Hardcoded fallback for polsubcyclo(15,4) C4 quartic

Batch26 (sub_3f1a6df07c50459f8b9c5533a9d17116):
- Strategy A: S3×C4 compositum (new T-number vs batch23-25)
- Strategy B: abelian cyclotomic polsubcyclo(m,12) seeds, m=35,39,52,72
- Strategy C: polsubcyclo(m,24) direct degree-24 cyclotomic
- Strategy D: S3×V4 (same T as batch25, fill gaps)

Batch27 (sub_e8bfc5c8b00b48d8b8a857a914de057c):
- Strategy A: polcompositum(f12, x²-d) — gives T-number G×C2, new vs shift-trick
  A2 uses D4-quartics with 2 real roots → r=12 (new r for same T)
- Strategy B: polsubcyclo(17,8) × S3 cubic → C8×S3 degree-24 (new T)
- Strategy C: splitting-field of D4-quartic × S3 cubic → D4-regular×S3 (new T)
- Strategy D: S3×C4 compositum with x²-d → (S3×C4)×C2 (new T)
- Strategy E: cyclotomic m=65,156,195,260,312,390,420,455,504,520,546,630
